title: Program Review Annual Update Fine Arts SK F18 (Part 1)
text:
College of the Siskiyous
Annual Program Update - Fine Arts October 17, 2018
College of the Siskiyous Program Review utilizes a four year cycle comprised of three
annual and one full review. This Fall 2018 represents the initialization year (annual) for
the cycle. Next year the program will undertake the full review. The principal preparer of
each program review (Zach Zweigle), in consultation with the full-time (Jim Gilmore,
Sean Kenny), part-time (Alan Crockett, Ted Helard), staff (Amanda Thomas) and
management of each program / area will complete the following Program Annual
Update. All annual updates shall be completed no later than December 15 of each year.
1. Describe annual progress / changes / events in the program.
The Fine Arts program made progress in the last year by successfully offering the
Design and Sculpture courses allowing the district to support students in completing
their educational goals and fulfilling requirements for their degrees. For some time prior
these classes have suffered from scheduling challenges and low enrollments.
Last year (2017-2018) the Fine Arts program at the college produced 65.05 FTES, or
$360,832.35 of state revenue. With a tenured faculty retirement pending (unofficial) as
most likely occurring in the next three semesters, and the recent retirement of tenured
faculty, Dennis Weathers in August, 2015, in addition to the reduction of the
Instructional Student Specialist II (ISS II) position to part time starting in June, 2012 (this
last reduction in staffing occurred during the country’s economic downturn) the art
program is taxed in both staffing and budgets, and struggling to maintain its standards
of health and success. All this, combined with ongoing annual reductions in institutional

-- 1 of 4 --

title: Program Review Annual Update Fine Arts SK F18 (Part 2)
text:
support and instructional budgets, is making our success a tougher and tougher goal to
achieve. These retirements and staff reductions represent huge cost savings to the
district totalling more than a quarter of a million dollars. Despite these many and
significant reasons (program budget cuts and staffing reductions) for a program to be
lacking in success, the art program continues to remain healthy and viable.
Many examples exist that demonstrate how the program is successful. The fine art
exhibit spaces around the Weed campus like the McCloud Galleries and the LRC Art
Gallery, in which student art exhibits are the predominant focus, are very popular for
guides showing the college to prospective students and parents, and provide ongoing
interest for our attending student body and staff. This program produces 12 to 16
exhibits per semester.
Interdisciplinary bridging to music in club activities and hosting of food events and music
activities using the programs student produced pizza oven promotes student success
through community. This program is one of the main hubs of student activity on the
Weed campus. The emphasis on community and belonging are integral to its success.
Students want access to studios, materials and each other as avenues to intellectual
and personal growth. The ISS II position supports these student interests/needs. The
COS art program represents opportunities for immersion into creative processes that
students find fascinating and affirming. It represents an area where students’ lives are
changed for the better; lessons learned in the art classes at this college help to build
functional, productive members of society.
Critiques in the many art courses offered at the college have performance components
that find students creating unique formats in presentation and conceptual projects. The
lack of full time ISS II support has had a negative effect on student success in the above
areas because of the reduction of the position. Our interest is not just in maintaining, but
in growing the success, health, standards, and stability of the College of the Siskiyous

-- 2 of 4 --

title: Program Review Annual Update Fine Arts SK F18 (Part 3)
text:
Fine Arts program. This concept currently sits at odds with the districts level of support
for fine arts at this college.
Our immediate need priority is in making our ISS II position full time again. It was a full
time position for over thirty years, and a bustling program like the College of the
Siskiyous Fine Arts program needs full time support to help push standards through
innovative approaches to teaching, art making and learning. Students rely heavily on
the ISS person for assistance with materials and equipment, access to studio time with
support, and guidance through their creative processes. A spirit of creativity and
progressiveness should exist at every level of this programs educational processes, and
a reinstated full time ISS II position will directly address this need.
2. Describe progress on assessing SLOs and PLOs for the program.
We have little to no direction on how this is supposed to be happening, but are excited
to participate.
3. Discuss any support or obstacles encountered by the program.
For SLO assessment, this year (18-19) is deemed the “initialization year” in the SLO
manual, so faculty are supposed to work with the SLO committee to create program
maps, make any necessary changes to program-level outcomes, and develop their first
key assessment (to be implemented next year. Unfortunately, there is no
currently-functioning SLO committee.
Other Fine Art program obstacles:
● Awkwardly scheduled classes
● Overlapping classes (Some of these overlaps make adequate, focused student
support from the ISS person very challenging.)
● Aging facilities

-- 3 of 4 --

● Outdated equipment in all areas of the program (For instance, our outdated color
printer which is relied on heavily by both students and staff, and our woodworking
equipment)
● Facilities improvements low priority for the college
● Dangerous work environment (first aid kits in studios non existent or not
replenished annually, fire extinguishers?, outdated and dangerous woodworking
equipment, window blinds not functioning and dangerous to room occupants,
non-institutionalized maintenance and cleaning for studio classes - being
required to request student worker money each year to have facilities maintained
and cleaned to reduce health hazards)
● Understaffed in aides and student workers
● Cuts in essential areas of budget (for instance, the cut of our hazardous waste
disposal funds, without which the photo lab cannot function)
● Unsure of our budget so it’s difficult to plan
● Unsure of our institutions priorities so again, It is difficult to plan
4. Describe program budgetary needs or implications. (Submit a CQIP if you are
requesting a budget allocation that falls outside of regular yearly budget allocations).
The cost of making the ISS II position from a 28 hour a week position to a full time
budgeted position would be approximately $35,00.00 dollars in salary and $25,000.00 in
benefits, retirement, etc.. Considering the FTES production of our program, reinstating a
full time art program ISS II position would not significantly impact the area’s bottom line,
and would be a huge benefit to the program and consequent student success.

-- 4 of 4 --
